rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Monday, March 3, 2025, Line 6, 1 - ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE RE: DISMISSAL PURSUANT TO CCP SECTION 391.4. Hearing required. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Person Most Qualified Depositions And Responsive Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Monday, March 3, 2025, Line 5, (PART 1 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING) PLAINTIFF JOHN DOE A MINOR, BY AND THROUGH HIS GUARDIAN AD LITEM, CAROL SANTONI-COSTANTINI'S Motion To Compel Person Most Qualified Depositions And Responsive Documents. Plaintiff John Doe's motion to compel person most qualified depositions and responsive documents is granted. Doe, a high-school student alleges that defendant San Francisco Unified School District failed to protect him from assaults by other students. Doe now seeks to compel the deposition of, and production of documents by, SFUSD's PMQ concerning various topics. These topics, and the documents that are sought related to these topics, touch on information about other students. The parties agree that SFUSD's records of students are protected by federal and state education-privacy laws and cannot be produced without court order. For good cause shown in the moving papers, the court grants Doe's motion and orders SFUSD to comply with the subpoena, including its documents requests, except that SFUSD may redact personally identifiable information of third-party students who are not the "assailant," as that term is defined in the deposition notice. The court orders that any information disclosed pursuant to the subpoena that is subject to federal and state education-privacy laws shall be restricted as follows: It shall be designated confidential; it may be used by Plaintiff's counsel only in connection with this litigation; it may be disclosed only to the parties, their attorneys, and any experts retained in connection with the litigation; before disclosure to any of the aforementioned persons, the receiving person must agree to be bound by the terms of this protective order; it must be destroyed within 90 days after this case is finally concluded; and if the court must consider any confidential information in order to rule on a motion or other request for relief, that information must be lodged under seal and accompanied by a motion to seal pursuant to CRC 2.550 and 2.551. SFUSD's time to comply and make production of documents shall be 20 calendar days after entry of this order, or the next business day thereafter if the date falls on a weekend or holiday. Doe asks the court not to give time for SFUSD to notify parents or guardians of students whose private information is subject to disclosure. But disclosure to parents or guardians is required under applicable statutes; this order does not override those obligations. SFUSD asks the court to receive parent/guardian objections in the first instance. The court denies that request. Parents/guardians may move to quash the subpoena and SFUSD is given leave to make a further motion to quash in accord with any parent/guardian objections it receives. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. VS. SAUL BAMACA
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Monday, March 3, 2025, Line 2 (Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling) PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Having considered the moving papers, and for good cause shown, Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'s unopposed motion for summary judgment is granted. "A party may move for summary judgment in an action or proceeding if it is contended that the action has no merit or that there is no defense to the action or proceeding." (CCP 437c(a)(1).) "The motion for summary judgment shall be granted if all the papers submitted show that there is no triable issue as to any material fact and that the moving party is entitled to a judgment as a matter of law." (CCP 437c(c).) The moving party must make an initial showing, based upon affirmative evidence, that no triable issue of material fact exists. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Company (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 850.) If the party carries this "burden of production, [the party] causes a shift, and the opposing party is then subjected to a burden of production of his own to make a prima facie showing of the existence of a triable issue of material fact." (Id.) The court finds that Wells Fargo has carried its burden of making an initial showing, based on affirmative evidence, that no triable issue of fact exists on any of its six causes of action. These causes of action are all based on a credit card agreement between Wells Fargo and defendant Saul Bamaca. Wells Fargo's evidence shows that Bamaca received the card along with a customer agreement (Ex. 2 to Velen Dec.; Peterson Dec. paras. 11-12); Defendant incurred charges on the card (Peterson Dec. para. 15 & Ex. 2 to Peterson Dec.); there are no charge disputes that are unresolved (Ex. 2 to Velen Dec.; Peterson Dec. para. 20); and Bamaca failed to pay a balance in the amount of $19,281.96 (Ex. 2 to Velen Dec; Peterson Dec. para. 22). Because Wells Fargo has carried its burden of providing affirmative evidence that no triable issue of material fact exists, the burden shifts to Bamaca to make a prima facie showing that a triable issue of material fact exists. Bamaca has not opposed the motion and has not carried this burden. Accordingly, the court grants summary judgment to Wells Fargo. It is entitled to judgment in the amount of $19,281.96. Wells Fargo has lodged a proposed judgment and seeks costs with its judgment. It has filed a memorandum of costs. Rule of Court 3.1700 states, "A prevailing party who claims costs must serve and file a memorandum of costs within 15 days after the date of service of the notice of entry of judgment or dismissal by the clerk under Code of Civil Procedure section 664.5 or the date of service of written notice of entry of judgment or dismissal, or within 180 days after entry of judgment, whichever is first." The court denies without prejudice Wells Fargo's premature request for costs and orders Wells Fargo to submit a proposed judgment with its proposed order that conforms to this ruling. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: MARLAYNA ALYSSA JACKSON VS. FCA US, LLC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Monday, March 3, 2025, Line 3, (PART 1 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING) DEFENDANT FCA US, LLC's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT. Defendant FCA US, LLC's demurrer to the sixth cause of action in Plaintiff's complaint, a claim for fraudulent inducement to contract by concealment, is overruled. The elements of this claim are "(1) misrepresentation (false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure); (2) knowledge of falsity (scienter); (3) intent to defraud (i.e., to induce reliance); (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) resulting damage." (Dhital v. Nissan North America, Inc. (2022) 84 Cal.App.5th 828, 845 (Dhital). FCA correctly argues that fraud must be pleaded with particularity. FCA contends that the sixth cause of action is insufficiently particular because it does not allege the "how, when, where, to whom, and by what means" the allegedly fraudulent representations were tendered. (Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 74 [italics omitted].) But FCA does not address Dhital, a case considering the adequacy of similar pleadings. In Dhital, plaintiffs brought a Song-Beverly Act claim against a car manufacturer. Along with Song-Beverly claims, they alleged "that Nissan, by intentionally concealing facts about the defective transmission, fraudulently induced them to purchase a car." (Dhital, 84 Cal.App.5th at p. 838.) The court of appeal held that plaintiffs adequately pleaded their fraudulent inducement claim by pleading that "the CVT transmissions installed in numerous Nissan vehicles (including the one plaintiffs purchased) were defective; Nissan knew of the defects and the hazards they posed; Nissan had exclusive knowledge of the defects but intentionally concealed and failed to disclose that information; Nissan intended to deceive plaintiffs by concealing known transmission problems; plaintiffs would not have purchased the car if they had known of the defects; and plaintiffs suffered damages in the form of money paid to purchase the car." (Id. at p. 844.) Plaintiff's fraudulent inducement claims contain similar allegations at similar levels of detail. (Compl. paras. 65-72; para. 18 ["Plaintiff purchased the Subject Vehicle as manufactured with FCA's defective engine."].) Dhital applies the established rule that when fraud occurs by omission or concealment, a plaintiff is not required to describe with particularity every conversation with a defendant's agent when the disclosure could or should have happened. (See Alfaro v. Community Housing Improvement System & Planning Assn., Inc. (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 1356, 1384.) FCA's reliance in its reply brief on Committee on Children's Television, Inc. v. General Foods Corp. (1983) 35 Cal.3d 197, is unavailing; in that case the Supreme Court excused the plaintiffs from describing each sugary-cereal advertisement alleged to be fraudulent with particularity, in light of the volume of advertisements and the fact that the defendants were better positioned to know what ads aired and when. (Id. at p. 217.) =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: GABRIEL ESPINOZA VS. BUMBLE BEE FOODS, LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And Consent Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Monday, March 3, 2025, Line 4, PLAINTIFF GABRIEL ESPINOZA's Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And Consent Judgment. The court continues the matter on its own motion to April 4, 2025. Plaintiff's motion to approve Proposition 65 settlement was filed on January 13, 2025. On February 11, 2025, Plaintiff served a Notice of Related Case concerning Chemical Toxin Working Group, Inc. v. Bumble Bee Foods LLC, L.A. Superior Court No. 23STCV17896. Plaintiff is ordered to renotice its motion to approve the Proposition 65 settlement for April 4, 2025, and to serve the parties in the related case with its moving papers. Plaintiff is further ordered to submit a short supplemental brief no later than 10 court days before the renoticed hearing, served on the parties in the related case and the Attorney General along with opposing counsel in this case, explaining why the proposed penalty amount is reasonable.
